title: Ladder Report
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
metrics <- load_spacer_metrics()
dat <- make_process_data(metrics)
predictors <- make_predictor_set()
model <- fit_or_load_joint_glmer(dat, predictors = predictors, refresh = params$refresh_model)
terms <- tidy_glmer(model)

title: Ladder Report
text:
1 Result Template


Code
knitr::kable(ladder_result_template())









question
current_answer




Do repairs happen?
Yes, but occurrence alone is not the target.


Do predictors align across process?
Test with process-by-predictor interactions in one joint model.


Would alignment imply one controller?
No. Alignment would be compatible with one profile, but the task design still separates production monitoring from offline text editing.


Would divergence matter philosophically?
Yes. Divergence supports the diagnostic warning that stable recovery can result from neighboring but non-identical stabilizers.

title: Ladder Report
text:
2 Current Model Signal


Code
signal <- terms |>
  dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "processspeaker:")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    direction = dplyr::case_when(
      estimate > 0 ~ "larger speaker slope",
      estimate < 0 ~ "smaller speaker slope",
      TRUE ~ "no contrast"
    )
  ) |>
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(abs(estimate)))
knitr::kable(signal, digits = 3)

title: Ladder Report
text:
3 Provisional Warrant
On the default SPACER metric surface, the workflow should be read as a test of profile alignment. A large process-by-predictor contrast counts against the simple story that speaker self-repair and offline comprehender correction instantiate one shared correction profile.
That result does not show that the speaker-side process is homeostatic. Nor does it show that the comprehender-side task is a controller. The stronger philosophical use is negative and diagnostic: SPACER makes it possible to distinguish recovery of intelligibility from evidence for a single corrective-control architecture.

title: Ladder Report
text:
4 Ladder Placement


Code
placement <- tibble::tribble(
  ~rung, ~status, ~reason,
  "Projection target", "Met", "The workflow declares a profile-comparison target before modeling.",
  "Stable profile", "Testable", "Repeated items and responses allow correction profiles to be estimated.",
  "Network order", "Partial", "Predictor ordering is plausible but not established as causal order.",
  "Maintenance", "Partial", "Speaker repair and comprehender correction are stabilizing pathways, but they are not one pathway.",
  "Corrective control", "Not warranted from SPACER alone", "The dataset supports profile comparison, not direct evidence for one online controller."
)
knitr::kable(placement)










rung
status
reason




Projection target
Met
The workflow declares a profile-comparison target before modeling.


Stable profile
Testable
Repeated items and responses allow correction profiles to be estimated.


Network order
Partial
Predictor ordering is plausible but not established as causal order.


Maintenance
Partial
Speaker repair and comprehender correction are stabilizing pathways, but they are not one pathway.


Corrective control
Not warranted from SPACER alone
The dataset supports profile comparison, not direct evidence for one online controller.

title: Ladder Report
text:
5 Paper Use
The cleanest use in the paper is as an empirical extension or appendix:

SPACER shows how the ladder changes the empirical question. The issue is not whether correction occurs, but whether the same perturbation measures predict speaker self-repair and comprehender correction in the same way. Divergence supports the diagnostic caution that intelligible recovery can be maintained by neighboring stabilizers rather than by one specified corrective-control system.

title: Model Checks
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
metrics <- load_spacer_metrics()
dat <- make_process_data(metrics)
predictors <- make_predictor_set()
model <- fit_or_load_joint_glmer(dat, predictors = predictors, refresh = params$refresh_model)
The model should reproduce basic features of the data before its process contrasts are interpreted.

title: Model Checks
text:
1 Observed and Fitted Rates


Code
checks <- observed_vs_predicted(model, dat)
knitr::kable(checks, digits = 3)

plot_observed_predicted(checks)

title: Model Checks
text:
2 Simulated Correction Rates
The following check simulates replicated outcomes from the fitted mixed model and compares process-level rates with the observed rates.


Code
sim_rates <- simulate_process_rates(model, nsim = 100)
plot_simulated_rates(sim_rates, dat)

title: Model Checks
text:
3 What This Does Not Check
These checks are intentionally modest. They do not validate the lexical metrics, the offline comprehender task, or a causal interpretation of the slopes. They only ask whether the fitted model can reproduce broad correction rates before the interactions are used in the ladder report.

title: Fake Data Recovery
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
shared <- simulate_correction_profiles(shared_profile = TRUE)
distinct <- simulate_correction_profiles(shared_profile = FALSE)
fit_shared <- fit_fake_recovery(shared)
fit_distinct <- fit_fake_recovery(distinct)
Fake-data simulation checks whether the proposed model can recover the distinction that matters before it is pointed at SPACER.

title: Fake Data Recovery
text:
1 Shared Profile Scenario
In the shared-profile simulation, the process-by-predictor interactions are set to zero.

  tibble::rownames_to_column("term") |>
  dplyr::filter(stringr::str_detect(term, "processspeaker:"))

title: Fake Data Recovery
text:
3 Check
The fake-data exercise is not evidence about SPACER. Its role is procedural: it checks that the model target is capable of distinguishing “same correction profile” from “different correction profile” under known conditions.

title: Declare the Case
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
The diagnostic question has to be declared before the model is fit. Otherwise the analysis risks treating any successful prediction of correction as evidence for control.

title: Declare the Case
text:
1 Case Declaration


Code
knitr::kable(ladder_declaration())










diagnostic_step
declaration_for_spacer
evidence_or_limit




Projection target
Predict when an erroneous lexical item is corrected, and whether production and comprehension support the same correction profile.
The target is a profile comparison, not a general claim that utterance correction is homeostatic.


Stable profile
A stable correction profile would show repeatable associations between lexical perturbation measures and correction probability.
Evidence comes from repeated items and comprehender responses in SPACER.


Network order
Candidate ordering runs from contextual fit, lexical similarity, and phonemic distance to correction decisions.
These are predictors of correction, not by themselves causal ordering relations.


Maintenance
Speaker self-repair and comprehender correction are candidate stabilizers of intelligible utterance recovery.
The comprehender task is offline text editing, so its pathway is not the same as online conversational repair.


Corrective control
The strongest claim would require perturbation-sensitive correction preserving a specified higher-scale relation.
SPACER can test profile alignment or divergence; it should not be read as direct evidence for one shared controller.

title: Declare the Case
text:
2 Estimand
The estimand is the contrast between two correction profiles:


Code
estimand <- tibble::tribble(
  ~component, ~declaration,
  "Outcome", "Whether a correction occurred.",
  "Process contrast", "Speaker self-repair versus offline comprehender correction.",
  "Predictors", "Semantic distance, phonemic distance, contextual predictability proxy, frequency proxy, and related lexical controls.",
  "Main quantity", "Process-by-predictor contrasts in predicted correction probability.",
  "Interpretation", "Aligned profiles support a shared correction profile; divergent profiles support distinct stabilizers."
)
knitr::kable(estimand)









component
declaration




Outcome
Whether a correction occurred.


Process contrast
Speaker self-repair versus offline comprehender correction.


Predictors
Semantic distance, phonemic distance, contextual predictability proxy, frequency proxy, and related lexical controls.


Main quantity
Process-by-predictor contrasts in predicted correction probability.


Interpretation
Aligned profiles support a shared correction profile; divergent profiles support distinct stabilizers.

title: Declare the Case
text:
3 Warrant Conditions
An aligned coefficient profile would not by itself prove a shared controller. SPACER separates the speaker’s original production process from an offline text-editing task. The most the workflow can show directly is whether the profiles align under these measurements.
A divergent profile is philosophically more diagnostic. It would support the paper’s caution that apparent preservation of intelligible utterance recovery can be produced by neighboring stabilizers rather than by one specified corrective-control architecture.

title: SPACER Diagnostic Workflow
text:
This workflow is a companion analysis scaffold for the diagnostic ladder in Not Every Stable Cluster Is Homeostatic. It uses SPACER, a parallel dataset of spontaneous speech-production errors and offline comprehender corrections, to ask a narrow question:
That question is deliberately weaker than “is utterance correction homeostatic?” The workflow treats homeostasis as the last rung of the ladder, not as a label attached once repair is observed.

title: SPACER Diagnostic Workflow
text:
1 Workflow

Declare the case before modeling.
Explore the data structure and the candidate predictors.
Simulate fake data under shared-profile and distinct-profile scenarios.
Fit a joint process-by-predictor model.
Check the model against observed correction patterns.
Run a small multiverse over reasonable coding choices.
Produce a ladder report that states what rung is warranted.

title: SPACER Diagnostic Workflow
SPACER Diagnostic Workflow
text:
3 Render
quarto render
The default model is a joint lme4::glmer() fit. A heavier brms model is included in the joint-model page and can be enabled when the local Stan backend is ready.

title: SPACER Diagnostic Workflow
text:
4 Statistical posture
The workflow follows a Gelman-style sequence: declare the estimand, plot the data, use partial pooling where possible, test recovery on fake data, check the fitted model, and expose the sensitivity of the conclusion to reasonable analytic choices (Gelman et al. 2020; Steegen et al. 2016).
The estimand is not a null-hypothesis verdict. It is the difference between production and comprehension correction profiles, especially process-by-predictor contrasts on the probability scale.

title: Explore the Data
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
raw <- load_spacer_raw()
metrics <- load_spacer_metrics()
dat <- make_process_data(metrics)

2 Modeling Surface
The joint analysis uses the SPACER metric files, reshaped into a long process-response table.

  dplyr::group_by(process) |>
  dplyr::summarise(
    rows = dplyr::n(),
    correction_rate = mean(corrected),
    items = dplyr::n_distinct(itemCrit),
    subjects = dplyr::n_distinct(subject),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

title: Explore the Data
text:
3 Predictor Distributions


Code
plot_predictor_distribution(dat, "phonemic_z", "Phonemic distance (z)")











Code

plot_predictor_distribution(dat, "surprisal_z", "Target minus error surprisal (z)")

title: Explore the Data
text:
4 Caution
The speaker rows and comprehender rows are parallel analysis rows, not identical observations of one online interaction. This is why the workflow asks about aligned profiles rather than immediately treating alignment as evidence for one controller.

title: Fit the Joint Model
text:
Code
source("R/setup.R")
metrics <- load_spacer_metrics()
dat <- make_process_data(metrics)
predictors <- make_predictor_set()
model <- fit_or_load_joint_glmer(dat, predictors = predictors, refresh = params$refresh_model)
The default fit is a joint logistic mixed model:
Code
formula(model)


corrected ~ process * (semantic_z + phonemic_z + frequency_z + 
    surprisal_z) + (1 | uttrID) + (1 | critWindow) + (1 | subject_key)
<environment: 0xb67fe2e40>
The process-by-predictor interactions are the main quantities. They test whether a predictor has a different association with speaker repair than with comprehender correction.

title: Fit the Joint Model
text:
3 Prediction Curves
These are population-level predictions with random effects held out.


Code
phonemic_grid <- make_prediction_grid(model, "phonemic_z", predictors)
plot_prediction_grid(phonemic_grid, "Phonemic distance (z)")











Code
surprisal_grid <- make_prediction_grid(model, "surprisal_z", predictors)
plot_prediction_grid(surprisal_grid, "Target minus error surprisal (z)")

title: Fit the Joint Model
text:
4 Bayesian Fit
The Bayesian model is the preferred final analysis if the workflow becomes a paper appendix or companion repository. It is off by default because it requires a working Stan backend and takes longer to render.


Code
bayes_model <- fit_joint_brms(
  dat,
  predictors = predictors,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  cores = 4,
  backend = "cmdstanr"
)
bayes_model


The priors are weakly informative on the log-odds scale. Before using the Bayesian fit for publication, add prior predictive checks and compare posterior predictive summaries against the observed correction rates.

  ggplot2::geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  ggplot2::geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "Process interaction estimate", y = NULL, color = "Phonemic measure")

title: Multiverse
text:
2 Interpretation Rule
The multiverse is not a pile of robustness badges. Its job is to identify whether the ladder conclusion depends on fragile coding choices. If the process-by-phonemic-distance contrast persists across reasonable variants, it is a strong candidate for the report. If a contrast appears only under one coding path, it should be treated as exploratory.
